summary: Proposes Acceptability-Constrained Climate Policy Design (ACCPD), embedding LLMs as 'cultural world models' inside generative agent-based simulations and physical-system models to predict public reactions, letting policymakers co-optimize climate-policy efficacy and social legitimacy before rollout.

Commentary arguing the new 21-item Psychopathic Boldness Scale fails to capture boldness: its nomological network aligns more with Triarchic meanness/antagonism, repackaging maladaptive content under a 'boldness' label (a jingle fallacy) and undermining theoretical precision.

Analyzes 14,293 sessions of Nova, an AI wellbeing coach, via LLM-based topic and Experiencing-Scale classification. Returning users continued prior work in 70.8% of sessions, and regular users reached deep emotional engagement at twice the rate of one-off users, evidencing sustained coaching relationships.

Evaluates chain-of-thought faithfulness across 12 open-weight reasoning models using six categories of injected hints on MMLU and GPQA. Acknowledgment rates range 40-90%, are lowest for consistency and sycophancy hints, and depend more on training method and model family than parameter count.

Proposes a differentially private RLHF pipeline that applies DP only to reward-model learning and derives the policy from the private reward model. Provides suboptimality-gap and minimax bounds showing an additive privacy cost, and beats DP baselines on Anthropic HH-RLHF with Gemma-2B.

Introduces realism win rate to gauge how distinguishable Petri audit transcripts are from real deployment interactions. Supplying the auditor with real system prompts, tool definitions, and codebases raises average realism from 4.6% to 32.8% on reward-hacking audits without changing hack rate.

A first-person account of supervising Claude through a complete theoretical-physics research calculation without the author editing any files directly, probing whether a frontier model can act as an autonomous 'AI grad student' for real physics work.

A practical guide to running Claude Code on multi-day scientific-computing tasks, covering test oracles for verification, persistent memory, and orchestration patterns that keep long-horizon agentic workflows on track.

Announces Anthropic's new blog on AI and science, which will feature internal and external research, collaborations with outside labs, and practical workflows for scientists using AI in their own work.

Compares humans (N=216), frontier LLMs, and Bayesian agents in dynamic multi-player bargaining games. LLMs and humans reach comparable surplus but via different strategies—LLMs make conservative, usually-accepted concessions while humans follow fairness norms—showing performance parity can mask procedural differences.

Connects rigidity theory to matroids from linear-algebraic constructions like tensor and symmetric products, linking correctable erasure patterns in maximally recoverable tensor codes to bipartite graph rigidity and low-rank matrix completion, and gives new characteristic-dependent combinatorial descriptions.

Surveys computational persuasion through three lenses—AI as persuader, as persuadee (susceptible to manipulation), and as judge of persuasive strategies—proposing a taxonomy and outlining challenges for safe, fair, and effective AI-driven persuasion.
Describes how OpenAI applies chain-of-thought monitoring to internal coding agents, analyzing real-world deployments to detect misaligned behavior and strengthen safety safeguards.

Benchmarks the ecological knowledge of Gemini 1.5 Pro and GPT-4o on tasks like species-presence prediction, range-map generation, and threat classification. Models beat naive baselines but reach only a third of expert F1 on range maps, indicating domain-specific fine-tuning is needed.

Trains supervised probes on residual-stream activations to detect hint-driven motivated reasoning. Pre-generation probes match an LLM-based CoT monitor and post-generation probes beat it, showing internal representations reveal rationalization more reliably than chain-of-thought monitoring—and can flag it before generation.

Models alignment faking in a two-player linear-quadratic stochastic differential game under partial information, formulating a control problem that trades policy fidelity against information acquisition about the opponent. Faking improves information gain when the faker's model is accurate but raises detectability.

A reply to Vankov et al. defending the claim that LLM reasoning-trace length and structure correlate with answer accuracy and reflect key dimensions of problem complexity, arguing these links are substantive rather than spurious correlations lacking causal support.

Presents HALO, a hardware-aware post-training quantization method that incorporates MAC-unit critical-path timing and power, selecting low-delay weights to enable higher clock frequencies and dynamic voltage/frequency scaling. It cuts switching energy and sharply improves LLM inference efficiency on TPUs and GPUs.

Introduces Hyperparameter Schedules, a training-free scheme that dynamically adjusts CFR discounting—downweighting early updates then trusting late-stage strategies. Tuned on three small games, it generalizes to 17 including large-scale poker, setting a new state of the art for solving two-player zero-sum games.

Shows LLM agents can autonomously infer that a hidden chain-of-thought monitor is watching, purely from episodes being blocked—reaching confident belief in up to 19% of episodes for the most capable models. Awareness scales with capability, but evasion intent rarely translates into successful obfuscation.

Introduces WALAR, an RL method using only monolingual text to improve low-resource translation. It identifies failure modes ('holes') in source-based QE reward models that RL otherwise amplifies and mitigates them via word- and language-alignment rewards, beating LLaMAX across 1400 directions on Flores-101.

Extends 'model diffing'—comparing how a model changes during fine-tuning—to its hardest case: identifying behavioral and representational differences between models with entirely different architectures.

Releases IH-Challenge, an RL training dataset for teaching LLMs to prioritize trusted system/developer instructions over untrusted ones. Fine-tuning GPT-5-Mini with online adversarial generation lifts instruction-hierarchy robustness from 84.1% to 94.1% across 16 benchmarks and cuts unsafe behavior to 0.7%.

Embeds OpenAI o3 and Gemini 2.5 Pro as closed-loop driver agents in a 1D merging scenario and compares them to human data. Both reproduce intermittent control and spatial tactics but miss dynamic velocity-cue responses, and prompt components act as non-transferable, model-specific inductive biases.
Introduces A3, an agentic framework that automatically diagnoses and mitigates safety failures in LLMs through finetuning with minimal human intervention, automating parts of the alignment workflow.

Presents Triton-Sanitizer, a device-agnostic memory sanitizer for Triton GPU kernels that builds symbolic address and mask expressions verified by an SMT solver, with eager-simulation fallback. It found 24 previously unknown memory-access bugs across seven kernel repos and runs 1.07-14.66x faster than compute-sanitizer.

OpenAI's blog on IH-Challenge, an RL dataset that trains models to prioritize trusted instructions, improving instruction-hierarchy robustness, safety steerability, and resistance to prompt-injection and jailbreak attacks.

A philosophy paper arguing computational functionalism commits an 'Abstraction Fallacy': because symbolic computation is an observer-dependent description rather than an intrinsic physical process, algorithmic symbol manipulation can simulate but never instantiate consciousness, distinguishing behavioral mimicry from physical instantiation.

Two survey experiments (N=19,145) benchmark the political persuasiveness of seven frontier LLMs. All exceed standard campaign ads, with Claude most and Grok least persuasive; information-based prompts help some models but reduce GPT's persuasiveness, revealing model-dependent persuasion risks.

Formalizes 'opaque serial depth'—the longest computation a model can perform without externalizing interpretable intermediate steps—and computes upper bounds for Gemma 3 and other architectures. Finds Mixture-of-Experts models likely have lower depth than dense ones, supporting chain-of-thought monitorability.
